title: Psoriatic Arthritis
oneLiner: Inflammatory arthritis with psoriasis; causes joint pain, swelling, and skin/nail disease – often underdiagnosed, but treatable with DMARDs/biologics.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis** – in a pt with known PsA presenting with an acutely hot, swollen, painful monoarticular joint. **Single finding:** synovial fluid Gram stain + culture + high WCC; do NOT assume it's a flare until aspirated.
- **Generalised pustular psoriasis / erythroderma** – though strictly a skin emergency, can co-occur. **Finding:** widespread pustules on erythema OR diffuse erythema with systemic features (fever, tachycardia, hypothermia). Requires urgent dermatology + fluid resuscitation.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Joint pain + morning stiffness >30 minutes (inflammatory pattern)
  - Dactylitis (“sausage digit”) – diffuse swelling of an entire finger/toe
  - Enthesitis – pain at Achilles tendon, plantar fascia, patellar tendon
  - Axial pain – inflammatory back pain (improves with exercise, worse at rest)
  - Nail changes – pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Psoriatic plaques (scalp, extensor surfaces, gluteal cleft, umbilicus)
  - Nail pitting / oil-drop discolouration
  - Synovitis (often asymmetric, oligoarticular or polyarticular)
  - Entheseal tenderness (e.g., medial/lateral epicondyles, insertion of Achilles)
  - Dactylitic digit – ‘sausage’ swelling (warm, not erythematous)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Rheumatoid arthritis** – symmetric polyarthritis of MCP/PIP, +ve RF/anti-CCP, no dactylitis or nail pits
- **Osteoarthritis** – morning stiffness <30 min, no inflammation (WCC normal), Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes
- **Gout / pseudogout** – acute monoarticular flare (first MTP or knee), crystals on synovial fluid, uric acid elevated
- **Reactive arthritis** – history of diarrhoeal/urethral infection, conjunctivitis, urethritis, HLA-B27
- **Ankylosing spondylitis** – pure axial (no peripheral arthritis or dactylitis), +ve HLA-B27, less skin/nail involvement
- **Splitter:** Presence of **dactylitis** (sausage digit) strongly favours PsA over RA or OA. Also, **nail pitting** is almost pathognomonic for PsA.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - MSK exam: assess number of swollen/tender joints, dactylitis count, enthesitis sites
  - Skin/nail exam: psoriasis plaques, pitting, onycholysis
  - If monoarticular hot joint – consider joint aspiration (send for Gram stain, culture, crystals, WCC)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease, leukocytosis in flare)
  - ESR/CRP (often elevated, but can be normal)
  - Renal profile, LFTs (baseline before DMARDs)
  - Uric acid (if gout suspected)
  - Rheumatoid factor / anti-CCP (to rule out RA – both negative in PsA)
- **Imaging:**
  - Plain X-ray of affected joints (hands, feet, spine, SI joints) – look for erosions with “pencil-in-cup” deformity, periostitis, joint space narrowing
  - Ultrasound with power Doppler – useful for enthesitis and synovitis
  - MRI – for axial disease or if X-rays equivocal
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **CASPAR criteria**
  - ≥3 points from: psoriasis (2 points if present, 1 if history or family history), nail dystrophy (1), dactylitis (1), negative RF (1), juxta-articular new bone formation on X-ray (1)
  - Sensitivity ~90%
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid aspiration** in any acute monoarticular presentation in a pt with PsA – do not assume it’s a flare
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If septic arthritis suspected: aspirate joint, send cultures, start empiric antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin + gentamicin – see local protocol)
  - If acute gout: NSAID (naproxen, indometacin) or colchicine (after checking eGFR)
  - Analgesia: simple analgesia (paracetamol) +/- NSAID if not contraindicated (avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment, GI bleed risk)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (CASPAR, imaging, bloods)
  - Start DMARD if not already – typically **methotrexate** (7.5-20 mg orally/SC once weekly) if moderate-severe polyarticular or dactylitis unresponsive to NSAIDs
  - If mono/oligoarticular and NSAID failure: consider intra-articular corticosteroid (triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg for large joint, 20 mg for small)
  - If active axial disease: NSAID + consider physical therapy
  - Refer to rheumatology for DMARD/biologic initiation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat skin and nails with topical steroids + vitamin D analogues (e.g., calcipotriol) ± phototherapy
  - DMARDs: methotrexate, sulfasalazine (enteric-coated – start 500 mg BD, increase to 2 g daily), leflunomide (10-20 mg daily)
  - Biologics (if inadequate response to ≥2 DMARDs): adalimumab (40 mg SC fortnightly), etanercept (25 mg twice weekly or 50 mg weekly), secukinumab (150 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks then monthly), ustekinumab (45/90 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, then every 12 weeks)
  - Monitor for infections, TB screening before TNF inhibitors
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl 125 mL/h if dehydrated or nil by mouth for procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if immobile
  - Diet: regular, unless post-operative or nausea
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly if stable, more frequent if septic arthritis or systemic flare
sections.Drugs & Doses:
**First-line agents:**
- *NSAID*: naproxen 500 mg PO BD (max 1 g/day) or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO TDS (max 2.4 g/day) – use with PPI if >65 or GI risk
- *Methotrexate*: 7.5-20 mg PO/SC once weekly (start 7.5 mg, increase by 2.5-5 mg every 4-8 wk). **Must prescribe folic acid 5 mg once weekly (day after MTX)**
- *Intra-articular steroid*: triamcinolone acetonide – dose per joint: large (knee) 40 mg, medium (ankle) 20-30 mg, small (MCP) 5-10 mg
**Common alternatives:**
- *Sulfasalazine*: enteric-coated – 500 mg BD, increase to 2 g daily (if tolerated)
- *Leflunomide*: 10-20 mg PO daily
- *Biologics*: adalimumab 40 mg SC fortnightly; etanercept 50 mg SC weekly; secukinumab 150 mg SC weekly for 4wk then monthly; ustekinumab (weight-based) 45 mg (≤100 kg) or 90 mg (>100 kg) SC at wk 0, 4, then q12wk
**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- MTX: avoid in pregnancy, active infection, significant hepatic/renal impairment (eGFR <30), significant alcohol use; interacts with NSAIDs (reduce clearance)
- Sulfasalazine: avoid in sulfonamide allergy, G6PD deficiency
- TNF inhibitors: avoid in active TB, hepatitis B (check screening), avoid live vaccines
**Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
- MTX: reduce dose if eGFR 30-50; contraindicated if eGFR <30; check LFTs before each dose increase
- Leflunomide: caution in hepatic impairment; consider washout if ALT >3× ULN
**Most dangerous dose error:**
- **Methotrexate – prescribing daily instead of weekly** – can cause severe myelosuppression, mucositis, hepatotoxicity. If error occurs: give intravenous folinic acid (leucovorin), contact poisons centre.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic joint misdiagnosis
  - DMARD toxicity (MTX pneumonitis, hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression; sulfasalazine rash/agranulocytosis)
  - Acute uveitis (rare, but if associated with PsA can cause vision loss)
- **Chronic:**
  - Joint destruction and disability (erosions, ankylosis)
  - Cardiovascular disease (systemic inflammation increases risk of MI/stroke)
  - Osteoporosis (if on long-term corticosteroids)
  - Depression and reduced quality of life
sections.Prognosis:
- Chronic relapsing-remitting course; early recognition and DMARD/biologic therapy improves joint outcomes and quality of life.
- Patients have increased cardiovascular risk; manage modifiable risk factors (smoking, BP, lipids).
- Nail disease often persistent and difficult to treat.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine skin and nails** – even if pt doesn’t mention it; check scalp, gluteal cleft, umbilicus. PsA can appear years before psoriasis.
- **Ask about family history of psoriasis or PsA** – strong genetic component.
- **Check for dactylitis and enthesitis** – these are key differentiators from other inflammatory arthritides.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume an acutely hot, swollen joint in a PsA patient is a flare** – must aspirate to exclude septic arthritis.
- **Do not prescribe methotrexate daily** – fatal error. Always confirm “once a week” on prescription.
- **Do not start systemic corticosteroids for PsA flare** – can cause rebound psoriasis and do not modify disease course; use topical/ intra-articular instead.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Joint becomes hot, red, or swollen (especially if you did not aspirate)
  - New fever or chills
  - Worsening skin rash, especially with pustules or widespread redness
  - Difficulty mobilising or increasing pain despite simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Rheumatology clinic in 4-6 weeks (or earlier if new DMARD/biologic). Also, GP for skin review if needed.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate ≥2 mmol/L (arterial)
  - GCS <15 (any drop) – rule out MTX neurotoxicity or sepsis
  - Anaphylaxis reaction to biologic infusion (rare with SC but possible)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Suspected septic arthritis: escalate to orthopaedics for urgent washout (if poor response to antibiotics)
  - MTX overdose (daily dosing): contact rheumatology + poisons centre + give folinic acid
  - Pustular psoriasis/erythroderma: admit to dermatology, IV fluids, keep warm.

rawMarkdown:
## Psoriatic Arthritis
**One-liner:** Inflammatory arthritis with psoriasis; causes joint pain, swelling, and skin/nail disease – often underdiagnosed, but treatable with DMARDs/biologics.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic arthritis** – in a pt with known PsA presenting with an acutely hot, swollen, painful monoarticular joint. **Single finding:** synovial fluid Gram stain + culture + high WCC; do NOT assume it's a flare until aspirated.
- **Generalised pustular psoriasis / erythroderma** – though strictly a skin emergency, can co-occur. **Finding:** widespread pustules on erythema OR diffuse erythema with systemic features (fever, tachycardia, hypothermia). Requires urgent dermatology + fluid resuscitation.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Joint pain + morning stiffness >30 minutes (inflammatory pattern)
  - Dactylitis (“sausage digit”) – diffuse swelling of an entire finger/toe
  - Enthesitis – pain at Achilles tendon, plantar fascia, patellar tendon
  - Axial pain – inflammatory back pain (improves with exercise, worse at rest)
  - Nail changes – pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Psoriatic plaques (scalp, extensor surfaces, gluteal cleft, umbilicus)
  - Nail pitting / oil-drop discolouration
  - Synovitis (often asymmetric, oligoarticular or polyarticular)
  - Entheseal tenderness (e.g., medial/lateral epicondyles, insertion of Achilles)
  - Dactylitic digit – ‘sausage’ swelling (warm, not erythematous)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Rheumatoid arthritis** – symmetric polyarthritis of MCP/PIP, +ve RF/anti-CCP, no dactylitis or nail pits
- **Osteoarthritis** – morning stiffness <30 min, no inflammation (WCC normal), Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes
- **Gout / pseudogout** – acute monoarticular flare (first MTP or knee), crystals on synovial fluid, uric acid elevated
- **Reactive arthritis** – history of diarrhoeal/urethral infection, conjunctivitis, urethritis, HLA-B27
- **Ankylosing spondylitis** – pure axial (no peripheral arthritis or dactylitis), +ve HLA-B27, less skin/nail involvement
- **Splitter:** Presence of **dactylitis** (sausage digit) strongly favours PsA over RA or OA. Also, **nail pitting** is almost pathognomonic for PsA.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - MSK exam: assess number of swollen/tender joints, dactylitis count, enthesitis sites
  - Skin/nail exam: psoriasis plaques, pitting, onycholysis
  - If monoarticular hot joint – consider joint aspiration (send for Gram stain, culture, crystals, WCC)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease, leukocytosis in flare)
  - ESR/CRP (often elevated, but can be normal)
  - Renal profile, LFTs (baseline before DMARDs)
  - Uric acid (if gout suspected)
  - Rheumatoid factor / anti-CCP (to rule out RA – both negative in PsA)
- **Imaging:**
  - Plain X-ray of affected joints (hands, feet, spine, SI joints) – look for erosions with “pencil-in-cup” deformity, periostitis, joint space narrowing
  - Ultrasound with power Doppler – useful for enthesitis and synovitis
  - MRI – for axial disease or if X-rays equivocal
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **CASPAR criteria**
  - ≥3 points from: psoriasis (2 points if present, 1 if history or family history), nail dystrophy (1), dactylitis (1), negative RF (1), juxta-articular new bone formation on X-ray (1)
  - Sensitivity ~90%
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid aspiration** in any acute monoarticular presentation in a pt with PsA – do not assume it’s a flare

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If septic arthritis suspected: aspirate joint, send cultures, start empiric antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin + gentamicin – see local protocol)
  - If acute gout: NSAID (naproxen, indometacin) or colchicine (after checking eGFR)
  - Analgesia: simple analgesia (paracetamol) +/- NSAID if not contraindicated (avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment, GI bleed risk)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (CASPAR, imaging, bloods)
  - Start DMARD if not already – typically **methotrexate** (7.5-20 mg orally/SC once weekly) if moderate-severe polyarticular or dactylitis unresponsive to NSAIDs
  - If mono/oligoarticular and NSAID failure: consider intra-articular corticosteroid (triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg for large joint, 20 mg for small)
  - If active axial disease: NSAID + consider physical therapy
  - Refer to rheumatology for DMARD/biologic initiation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat skin and nails with topical steroids + vitamin D analogues (e.g., calcipotriol) ± phototherapy
  - DMARDs: methotrexate, sulfasalazine (enteric-coated – start 500 mg BD, increase to 2 g daily), leflunomide (10-20 mg daily)
  - Biologics (if inadequate response to ≥2 DMARDs): adalimumab (40 mg SC fortnightly), etanercept (25 mg twice weekly or 50 mg weekly), secukinumab (150 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks then monthly), ustekinumab (45/90 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, then every 12 weeks)
  - Monitor for infections, TB screening before TNF inhibitors
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl 125 mL/h if dehydrated or nil by mouth for procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if immobile
  - Diet: regular, unless post-operative or nausea
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly if stable, more frequent if septic arthritis or systemic flare

### Drugs & Doses
**First-line agents:**
- *NSAID*: naproxen 500 mg PO BD (max 1 g/day) or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO TDS (max 2.4 g/day) – use with PPI if >65 or GI risk
- *Methotrexate*: 7.5-20 mg PO/SC once weekly (start 7.5 mg, increase by 2.5-5 mg every 4-8 wk). **Must prescribe folic acid 5 mg once weekly (day after MTX)**
- *Intra-articular steroid*: triamcinolone acetonide – dose per joint: large (knee) 40 mg, medium (ankle) 20-30 mg, small (MCP) 5-10 mg
**Common alternatives:**
- *Sulfasalazine*: enteric-coated – 500 mg BD, increase to 2 g daily (if tolerated)
- *Leflunomide*: 10-20 mg PO daily
- *Biologics*: adalimumab 40 mg SC fortnightly; etanercept 50 mg SC weekly; secukinumab 150 mg SC weekly for 4wk then monthly; ustekinumab (weight-based) 45 mg (≤100 kg) or 90 mg (>100 kg) SC at wk 0, 4, then q12wk
**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- MTX: avoid in pregnancy, active infection, significant hepatic/renal impairment (eGFR <30), significant alcohol use; interacts with NSAIDs (reduce clearance)
- Sulfasalazine: avoid in sulfonamide allergy, G6PD deficiency
- TNF inhibitors: avoid in active TB, hepatitis B (check screening), avoid live vaccines
**Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
- MTX: reduce dose if eGFR 30-50; contraindicated if eGFR <30; check LFTs before each dose increase
- Leflunomide: caution in hepatic impairment; consider washout if ALT >3× ULN
**Most dangerous dose error:**
- **Methotrexate – prescribing daily instead of weekly** – can cause severe myelosuppression, mucositis, hepatotoxicity. If error occurs: give intravenous folinic acid (leucovorin), contact poisons centre.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic joint misdiagnosis
  - DMARD toxicity (MTX pneumonitis, hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression; sulfasalazine rash/agranulocytosis)
  - Acute uveitis (rare, but if associated with PsA can cause vision loss)
- **Chronic:**
  - Joint destruction and disability (erosions, ankylosis)
  - Cardiovascular disease (systemic inflammation increases risk of MI/stroke)
  - Osteoporosis (if on long-term corticosteroids)
  - Depression and reduced quality of life

### Prognosis
- Chronic relapsing-remitting course; early recognition and DMARD/biologic therapy improves joint outcomes and quality of life.
- Patients have increased cardiovascular risk; manage modifiable risk factors (smoking, BP, lipids).
- Nail disease often persistent and difficult to treat.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always examine skin and nails** – even if pt doesn’t mention it; check scalp, gluteal cleft, umbilicus. PsA can appear years before psoriasis.
- **Ask about family history of psoriasis or PsA** – strong genetic component.
- **Check for dactylitis and enthesitis** – these are key differentiators from other inflammatory arthritides.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume an acutely hot, swollen joint in a PsA patient is a flare** – must aspirate to exclude septic arthritis.
- **Do not prescribe methotrexate daily** – fatal error. Always confirm “once a week” on prescription.
- **Do not start systemic corticosteroids for PsA flare** – can cause rebound psoriasis and do not modify disease course; use topical/ intra-articular instead.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Joint becomes hot, red, or swollen (especially if you did not aspirate)
  - New fever or chills
  - Worsening skin rash, especially with pustules or widespread redness
  - Difficulty mobilising or increasing pain despite simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Rheumatology clinic in 4-6 weeks (or earlier if new DMARD/biologic). Also, GP for skin review if needed.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate ≥2 mmol/L (arterial)
  - GCS <15 (any drop) – rule out MTX neurotoxicity or sepsis
  - Anaphylaxis reaction to biologic infusion (rare with SC but possible)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Suspected septic arthritis: escalate to orthopaedics for urgent washout (if poor response to antibiotics)
  - MTX overdose (daily dosing): contact rheumatology + poisons centre + give folinic acid
  - Pustular psoriasis/erythroderma: admit to dermatology, IV fluids, keep warm.
